title: Chicken tagine with preserved lemon and olives
subtitle: Braised chicken thighs with saffron, green olives, and preserved lemon
excerpt: Bone-in chicken thighs braised in a saffron and ginger broth with green olives and preserved lemon until the meat is falling from the bone and the sauce has reduced to a concentrated, fragrant liquid. The classic Moroccan djej m'chermel, one of the great braised chicken dishes.
sourceNotes: Djej m'chermel (chicken with preserved lemon and olives) is a classic Moroccan dish documented in cookery accounts from the nineteenth century. Paula Wolfert's The Food of Morocco (1973) provides a thorough analysis of the technique. The preserved lemon and olive combination is central to Moroccan cookery and is described in multiple regional Moroccan sources.

content.text: Chicken cooks faster than lamb, which makes this tagine more approachable on a weekday than the lamb version, but it still benefits from a low, slow braise rather than a quick cook. The saffron, ginger, and turmeric build the sauce's colour and depth; the preserved lemon and olives are added near the end to keep their brightness intact.

Remove the skin from the chicken thighs before browning if you want a cleaner sauce; leave it on if you want the added richness. Preserved lemons are sold in jars in most supermarkets. Use the rind only, scrape out the flesh and discard it, then slice the rind finely.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: preserved, rind finely sliced, flesh discarded

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil in a dutch oven over medium-high heat. Season the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs with the {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Brown skin-side down for 5 to 6 minutes until deep golden. Turn and cook for 2 more minutes. Remove and set aside.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onions to the pot and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-ground}} of ginger, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin. Stir for 1 minute. Return the chicken to the pot. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of stock and the saffron water. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 40 minutes.
Add the olives and preserved lemon

Add the {{olives-green}} of olives and the sliced rind of the {{lemon}} preserved lemon. Cook uncovered for a further 15 minutes until the sauce has reduced slightly. Taste and adjust the salt, the preserved lemon is salty, so taste before adding more. Serve over couscous with {{parsley-flat}} of parsley scattered over.

items.fix: Take off the lid and simmer 15 to 20 minutes to reduce. Or lift out the chicken, reduce the sauce on its own, and slide the chicken back in
items.cause: Too much stock or lid not removed at the end

items.fix: Use the rind only, scraping out the flesh; start with half a preserved lemon and taste before adding more
items.cause: Too much lemon rind or flesh included

items.fix: Brown skin-side down in a very hot pan for a full 5 to 6 minutes without moving; the skin must be deep golden before turning
items.cause: Not browned long enough at the start
items.symptom: Chicken skin is flabby rather than coloured

content.text: Djej m'chermel is one of the signature dishes of the Moroccan table. The key ingredient is preserved lemon: lemons packed in salt and their own juice for at least a month. The rind softens and the flavour turns floral and sharp, nothing like fresh lemon. There is no close equivalent in Western cooking. Preserved lemon turns up in tagines, salads, and fish dishes across Morocco. The combination of preserved lemon, olives, saffron, and ginger is the signature of Fassi cooking from the city of Fez, often called the high point of Moroccan food.
